metadata.title: Docker Complete Learning Path
metadata.subtitle: Interactive DevOps Journey - From Zero to Production
metadata.description: Master Docker containerization from installation to orchestration with hands-on labs and real-world scenarios
learningObjectives: Install and configure Docker on any platform
Create custom Docker images with Dockerfiles

sections.title: What is Docker?
sections.content: Docker is a platform that packages your application and all its dependencies into a 'container' - think of it as a lightweight, portable box that runs the same everywhere.

items.title: Instant environment setup
items.description: New team members productive in minutes

items.title: Resource efficient
items.description: Unlike VMs, containers share the host OS kernel

sections.title: Key Insight
sections.content: Docker containers are NOT virtual machines. They're much lighter and faster!

steps.title: Download Docker Desktop
steps.description: Get the appropriate version for your OS

codeBlocks.title: Linux Installation
codeBlocks.code:
# For Windows/Mac: Visit docker.com/products/docker-desktop
# For Linux Ubuntu/Debian:
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com -o get-docker.sh
sudo sh get-docker.sh

steps.title: Verify Installation
steps.description: Check that Docker is properly installed

codeBlocks.title: Version Check
codeBlocks.code:
docker --version
# Expected: Docker version 24.x.x, build xxxxx

codeBlocks.title: Hello World Test
codeBlocks.code:
docker run hello-world
# This downloads a test image and runs it

steps.title: Enable Docker Service (Linux)
steps.description: Set up Docker to start automatically

codeBlocks.title: Service Setup
codeBlocks.code:
sudo systemctl enable docker
sudo systemctl start docker
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
# Log out and back in for group changes

sections.title: Success Indicator
sections.content: If you see 'Hello from Docker!' - you're ready to go!

codeBlocks.title: Fix Docker Daemon
codeBlocks.code:
# Fix: Start Docker service
sudo systemctl start docker
# Or on Mac/Windows: Open Docker Desktop app

codeBlocks.title: Fix Permissions
codeBlocks.code:
# Fix: Add user to docker group
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
newgrp docker
# Or logout/login

issues.solution: Enable WSL2 in Windows Features, install WSL2 kernel update from Microsoft
Enable virtualization in BIOS/UEFI settings (Intel VT-x or AMD-V)

questions.question: What is the main difference between Docker containers and virtual machines?
options: Containers share the host OS kernel, VMs have their own OS
VMs are more portable than containers
questions.explanation: Containers share the host operating system's kernel, making them much lighter and faster than VMs which require their own full OS.

questions.question: Docker Desktop is required for Linux installations.
questions.explanation: Docker Desktop is for Windows and Mac. Linux can install Docker Engine directly using the command line.

questions.question: The command to verify Docker installation is: docker ___
questions.explanation: Use 'docker --version' or 'docker -v' to check the installed Docker version.

sections.title: Container Fundamentals
sections.content: Containers are running instances of Docker images. Think of it this way:

items: Image = Recipe/Blueprint (like a class in programming)

items.title: Isolation
items.description: Each container has its own filesystem, network, process space

items.title: Layers
items.description: Images built in layers, containers add writable layer on top

items.title: Ephemeral
items.description: Containers are temporary by default - data lost on removal

codeBlocks.title: Image Management
codeBlocks.code:
# Download nginx web server image
docker pull nginx:latest

# List downloaded images
docker images

codeBlocks.title: Container Execution
codeBlocks.code:
# Run nginx in foreground (Ctrl+C to stop)
docker run nginx

# Run in background (detached)
docker run -d nginx

# Run with port mapping (host:container)
docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx
# Visit http://localhost:8080

codeBlocks.title: Container Management
codeBlocks.code:
# List running containers
docker ps

# List all containers (including stopped)
docker ps -a

# Stop a container
docker stop [container_id]

# Start a stopped container
docker start [container_id]

# Remove a container
docker rm [container_id]

codeBlocks.title: Container Interaction
codeBlocks.code:
# View logs
docker logs [container_id]
docker logs -f [container_id]
# Follow logs in real-time

# Execute commands inside container
docker exec -it [container_id] bash
# Type 'exit' to leave

# Copy files to/from container
docker cp file.txt [container_id]:/path/
docker cp [container_id]:/path/file.txt .

sections.title: Practice Exercise
sections.content: Practice: Run MongoDB with mongo-express UI!

codeBlocks.title: MongoDB Stack Setup
codeBlocks.code:
# Create network
docker network create mongo-network

# Run MongoDB
docker run -d \
    --name mongodb \
    --network mongo-network \
    -e MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME=admin \
    -e MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD=pass \
    mongo

# Run Mongo Express
docker run -d \
    --name mongo-express \
    --network mongo-network \
    -p 8081:8081 \
    -e ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINUSERNAME=admin \
    -e ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINPASSWORD=pass \
    -e ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_SERVER=mongodb \
    mongo-express

# Visit http://localhost:8081

codeBlocks.title: Debug Exit
codeBlocks.code:
docker logs [container_id]
# Look for error messages

issues.solution: Find what's using the port or use a different one

codeBlocks.title: Port Conflict Resolution
codeBlocks.code:
# Find what's using the port
sudo lsof -i :8080
# Linux/Mac
netstat -ano | findstr :8080
# Windows

# Use different port
docker run -p 8090:80 nginx

issues.solution: Check port mapping and container status
checklist: Ensure container is running: docker ps

codeBlocks.title: Cleanup Commands
codeBlocks.code:
# Clean up unused resources
docker system prune -a

# Remove stopped containers
docker container prune

# Remove unused images
docker image prune -a

issues.solution: Remove existing container or use different name

codeBlocks.title: Name Conflict Resolution
codeBlocks.code:
# Remove container with same name
docker rm [container_name]

# Or use auto-generated names
docker run nginx
# Docker assigns random name

questions.question: What is the difference between 'docker run' and 'docker start'?
options: 'docker run' creates a new container, 'docker start' starts an existing stopped container
'docker run' is for images, 'docker start' is for containers
questions.explanation: 'docker run' creates and starts a new container from an image, while 'docker start' restarts an existing stopped container.

questions.question: Complete the command to run nginx on port 8080
questions.explanation: The -p flag maps host port 8080 to container port 80.

questions.question: Which command shows only running containers?
questions.explanation: 'docker ps' shows only running containers. Add '-a' to see all containers including stopped ones.

sections.title: Understanding Docker Images
sections.content: Images are the blueprints for containers. They contain:

sections.title: Image Layers
sections.content: Each instruction in Dockerfile creates a new layer. Layers are cached and reused!

codeBlocks.title: Project Setup
Dockerfile
codeBlocks.code:
# Use official Node.js image
FROM node:18-alpine

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Copy package files first (layer caching!)
COPY package*.json ./

# Install dependencies
RUN npm install

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Expose port
EXPOSE 3000

# Define startup command
CMD ["npm", "start"]

codeBlocks.title: Build Process
codeBlocks.code:
# Build with tag
docker build -t my-node-app:1.0 .

# Watch the layers being created!
# Each step creates a new layer

# List your images
docker images

# Run your custom image
docker run -d -p 3000:3000 my-node-app:1.0
# Visit http://localhost:3000

codeBlocks.title: Debug Build
codeBlocks.code:
# Debug with interactive shell
docker run -it node:18-alpine sh

# Test commands manually first

issues.solution: Use alpine images, multi-stage builds, .dockerignore file

codeBlocks.title: .dockerignore
codeBlocks.code:
node_modules
.git
*.md
.env

codeBlocks.title: Specify Dockerfile
codeBlocks.code:
# Specify Dockerfile location
docker build -f path/to/Dockerfile -t myapp .

questions.question: Arrange these Dockerfile instructions in the optimal order for layer caching:
questions.explanation: Copy package files and install dependencies before copying application code to maximize layer cache reuse.

questions.question: What is the purpose of .dockerignore file?
options: To exclude files from being copied into the image
To hide containers from docker ps
questions.explanation: .dockerignore prevents specified files and directories from being copied into the Docker image during build.

sections.title: What is Docker Compose?
sections.content: Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications using a YAML file.

items.title: Declarative Configuration
items.description: Define entire application stack in a single file

items.title: Development Simplicity
items.description: One command to start entire application

items.title: Environment Consistency
items.description: Same setup across dev, test, and production

codeBlocks.title: Basic Compose File
codeBlocks.code:
version: '3.8'

services:
  web:
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
    volumes:
      - ./html:/usr/share/nginx/html
    depends_on:
      - api

  api:
    build: ./api
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=development
      - DB_HOST=database
    depends_on:
      - database

  database:
    image: postgres:13
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp
      POSTGRES_USER: user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"

volumes:
  postgres_data:

networks:
  default:
    name: myapp-network

codeBlocks.title: Compose Operations
codeBlocks.code:
# Start all services
docker-compose up

# Start in background
docker-compose up -d

# Build and start
docker-compose up --build

# Stop all services
docker-compose down

# Stop and remove volumes
docker-compose down -v

# View running services
docker-compose ps

# View logs
docker-compose logs
docker-compose logs web

# Execute commands in service
docker-compose exec web sh

codeBlocks.title: Production Compose
codeBlocks.code:
version: '3.8'

services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.prod
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:3000/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 5
    deploy:
      replicas: 3
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 512M
        reservations:
          memory: 256M
    networks:
      - app-network

  nginx:
    image: nginx:alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf
      - ./ssl:/etc/ssl/certs
    depends_on:
      - app
    networks:
      - app-network

networks:
  app-network:
    driver: bridge

volumes:
  app_data:
    driver: local

issues.solution: Use service names for internal communication

codeBlocks.title: Service Communication
codeBlocks.code:
# Correct: Use service name
DB_HOST=database

# Wrong: Use localhost
DB_HOST=localhost

codeBlocks.title: Port Mapping
codeBlocks.code:
ports:
  - "8080:80"  # host:container
  - "8081:80"  # different host port

codeBlocks.title: Service Dependencies
codeBlocks.code:
web:
  depends_on:
    database:
      condition: service_healthy
database:
  healthcheck:
    test: ["CMD", "pg_isready"]
    interval: 5s

questions.question: How do services communicate with each other in Docker Compose?
questions.explanation: Docker Compose creates a default network where services can communicate using service names as hostnames.

questions.question: Complete the volume mapping to persist database data:
questions.explanation: Named volumes persist data beyond container lifecycle.

sections.title: Production Considerations
sections.content: Moving from development to production requires attention to security, performance, monitoring, and reliability.

items.title: Security
items.description: Non-root users, secrets management, network policies

items.title: Reliability
items.description: Restart policies, backup strategies, rollback plans

sections.title: Production vs Development
sections.content: Different images, configurations, and infrastructure for production

codeBlocks.title: Production Dockerfile
codeBlocks.code:
# Multi-stage build for smaller image
FROM node:18-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production

# Production stage
FROM node:18-alpine AS production

# Create non-root user
RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S nodejs
RUN adduser -S nextjs -u 1001

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Copy built application
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app .

# Switch to non-root user
USER nextjs

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1

EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["npm", "start"]

codeBlocks.title: docker-compose.prod.yml
codeBlocks.code:
version: '3.8'

services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.prod
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=production
    env_file:
      - .env.prod
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 512M
          cpus: '0.5'
        reservations:
          memory: 256M
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:3000/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 40s
    logging:
      driver: "json-file"
      options:
        max-size: "10m"
        max-file: "3"
    networks:
      - app-network

  nginx:
    image: nginx:alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./nginx/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro
      - ./ssl:/etc/ssl/certs:ro
      - ./static:/var/www/static:ro
    depends_on:
      app:
        condition: service_healthy
    logging:
      driver: "json-file"
      options:
        max-size: "10m"
        max-file: "3"
    networks:
      - app-network

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: redis-server --requirepass ${REDIS_PASSWORD}
    volumes:
      - redis_data:/data
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 128M
    networks:
      - app-network

volumes:
  redis_data:
    driver: local

networks:
  app-network:
    driver: bridge
    ipam:
      config:
        - subnet: 172.20.0.0/16

codeBlocks.title: deploy.sh
codeBlocks.code:
#!/bin/bash
set -e

echo "🚀 Starting production deployment..."

# Pull latest images
docker-compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml pull

# Build application
docker-compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml build --no-cache

# Run database migrations
docker-compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml run --rm app npm run migrate

# Stop old containers
docker-compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml down

# Start new containers
docker-compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d

# Wait for health checks
echo "⏳ Waiting for services to be healthy..."
sleep 30

# Verify deployment
if docker-compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml ps | grep -q "Up (healthy)"; then
  echo "✅ Deployment successful!"
  # Clean up old images
  docker image prune -f
else
  echo "❌ Deployment failed!"
  docker-compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml logs
  exit 1
fi

echo "🎉 Production deployment complete!"

codeBlocks.title: Debug Production Issues
codeBlocks.code:
# Check container status
docker-compose ps

# View logs
docker-compose logs --tail=100 app

# Check resource usage
docker stats

# Inspect container
docker inspect [container_id]

codeBlocks.title: Security Scanning
codeBlocks.code:
# Scan images for vulnerabilities
docker scout quickview
docker scout cves myapp:latest

# Update base images regularly
docker pull node:18-alpine
docker-compose build --no-cache

questions.question: What is the main security benefit of multi-stage Docker builds?
options: Smaller final image with only production dependencies
questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds allow you to exclude development tools and dependencies from the final production image.

questions.question: Which restart policy is best for production applications?
questions.explanation: 'unless-stopped' ensures containers restart automatically unless explicitly stopped, ideal for production services.
